Title: Deficient plastidic fatty acid synthesis triggers cell death by modulating mitochondrial reactive oxygen species

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ROS acts in signaling for programmed cell death and defense
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SOM3 suppresses mod1 growth defects, ROS accumulation, and cell death by reducing complex I abundance/activity
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. som3 was identified as a recessive suppressor of mod1. 2. SOM3 corresponds to AT1G47260, a mitochondria-localized complex I subunit. 3. Loss of SOM3 reduces complex I NADH oxidase activity and suppresses mod1-associated ROS accumulation and programmed cell death.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA suppressor cloning; RT-PCR; complementation with 35S:SOM3 cDNA; second allele validation; GFP localization; western blot; in-gel NADH oxidase assay
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous illumination growth; pathogen assays used later for related complex I deficiency phenotypes
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complex I deficiency is associated with attenuated HR and increased bacterial susceptibility in related mutants; likely respiratory defects.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SOM3 is described as a subunit/component of mitochondrial ETC complex I rather than an enzyme or transcriptional regulator.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential handle for modulating mitochondrial ROS-dependent PCD, but practical use limited by probable respiration and immunity penalties.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ROS signaling in cell death and immunity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ndufs4-1 suppresses mod1 growth defects, cell death, and ROS accumulation through complex I deficiency; also strongly compromises bacterial resistance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NDUFS4 loss suppresses mod1 phenotypes. 2. The mutant shows reduced complex I activity correlated with lower ROS accumulation. 3. ndufs4-1 has severe susceptibility to bacteria and attenuated HR, indicating intact complex I is needed for defense.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic crossing with mod1; phenotype analysis; ROS staining; HR and bacterial growth assays
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pathogen infection with Pst DC3000 and avirulence strains
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Compromised basal and effector-triggered resistance; severe complex I deficiency.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A core experimentally tested complex I component in Results; stronger defense defects than other complex I-deficient mutants.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a favorable engineering target due to strong respiration and immunity penalties.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Cytosolic SOD activity assayed in seedlings; overexpression lines analyzed in mod1 background
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ROS acts in signaling and oxidative damage leading to PCD
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CSD1 overexpression reduces ROS and rescues mod1 developmental and cell death phenotypes
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 35S:CSD1 in mod1 largely rescues the mutant phenotype. 2. Rescue strength correlates with higher SOD activity and lower ROS accumulation. 3. These data support ROS as a causal trigger of mod1 cell death.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; SOD activity assay; ROS staining; phenotype and Trypan blue staining
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CSD1 is used functionally to scavenge ROS rather than as the primary mechanistic cause of the original defect.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antioxidant engineering target to reduce ROS-driven damage, though broader physiological consequences were not explored.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Respiratory burst oxidase homolog; plasma membrane NADPH oxidase
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of AtRBOHD, alone or with AtRBOHF, does not suppress mod1 cell death, indicating NADPH oxidase-derived ROS is not the causal source in mod1
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. atrbohD mod1 and atrbohD atrbohF mod1 retain mod1-like morphology and cell death. 2. NADPH oxidase-derived ROS is dispensable for mod1 cell-death signaling. 3. The causal ROS source is therefore distinct from plasma membrane RBOH activity.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double/triple mutant analysis; phenotype observation; Trypan blue and NBT staining
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because directly tested in Results as an alternative ROS source, but effect is negative/non-causal for mod1 suppression.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited here; demonstrates that targeting RBOHD alone will not block mod1-like mitochondrial ROS-PCD.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Respiratory burst oxidase homolog; plasma membrane NADPH oxidase
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of AtRBOHF together with AtRBOHD does not suppress mod1 cell death, indicating plasma membrane NADPH oxidase-derived ROS is not directly responsible
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. atrbohD atrbohF mod1 triple mutant resembles mod1 in morphology and cell death. 2. Blocking NADPH oxidase-derived ROS does not alter mod1 signaling outcome. 3. mod1 cell death mainly depends on mitochondrial ETC-derived ROS instead.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Triple mutant analysis; phenotype observation; Trypan blue and NBT staining
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a directly tested alternative ROS-source gene with negative evidence.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited in this context; indicates RBOHF-targeted manipulation is unlikely to suppress mod1-like PCD.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces trans-2-enoyl-ACP to saturated acyl-ACP during plastid fatty acid chain elongation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential membrane lipid building blocks and contributors to organelle function/signaling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of MOD1 function triggers ROS accumulation and programmed cell death; intact MOD1 negatively regulates PCD
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. mod1 mutant accumulates H2O2 and O2− and shows programmed cell death. 2. MOD1 encodes a chloroplast-localized enoyl-ACP reductase essential for plastidic fatty acid synthesis. 3. Defective MOD1 initiates a chloroplast-to-mitochondrion signal that depends on mitochondrial ETC/complex I to trigger PCD.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotype analysis; ROS staining (DAB/NBT); subcellular localization with GFP
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous illumination growth; pathogen challenge discussed for HR context
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for plastid fatty acid synthesis-linked PCD initiation, but rate-limiting enzyme not established
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MOD1 is the causal gene of the mod1 mutant used as the biological entry point of the study; the paper focuses on how its deficiency leads to mitochondrial ROS-dependent cell death rather than on nutrient biofortification.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct nutrient-engineering value from this paper; maintaining plastid fatty acid synthesis is important to avoid ROS-triggered growth defects and cell death.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes severe developmental defects and cell death.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ROS acts as a signal triggering programmed cell death and contributing to hypersensitive response/disease resistance
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Mitochondrial electron transport chain function via complex I transcript maturation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Post-transcriptional regulation of mitochondrial transcript maturation/splicing leading to reduced complex I accumulation/activity when overexpressed
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Specifically decreases mature NAD7 transcripts and NAD7 protein accumulation when overexpressed
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression of SOM42 suppresses mod1 growth defects, ROS accumulation, and cell death by reducing complex I activity
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Strong negative regulator of complex I-dependent mitochondrial ROS production in the mod1 context
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. som42 is a gain-of-function suppressor caused by T-DNA insertion in the AT2G01390 promoter. 2. SOM42 is a mitochondria-localized PPR protein that specifically impairs NAD7 transcript maturation. 3. SOM42 overexpression reduces complex I abundance/activity and suppresses mod1-associated ROS accumulation and programmed cell death.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Suppressor cloning; RT-qPCR; overexpression complementation; mitochondrial localization; northern blot; western blot for NAD7; Blue Native-PAGE/in-gel NADH oxidase assay
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Continuous illumination growth; pathogen infection assays for HR/resistance
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Gain-of-function T-DNA insertion in promoter; transgenic 35S overexpression
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Indirectly affects mitochondrial complex I through regulation of NAD7 maturation
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Possible feedback control inferred from altered NAD transcript levels, discussed but not directly proven
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate mitochondrial ROS and cell-death sensitivity, but likely accompanied by reduced respiration and defense capacity.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complex I deficiency compromises hypersensitive response, basal resistance, and resistance to virulent/avirulent bacteria.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not a transcription factor, SOM42 is a core regulator because it post-transcriptionally controls NAD7 maturation and thereby complex I-derived ROS.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ROS signal for programmed cell death and immune responses
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Mitochondrial complex I assembly/function via NAD7 intron 1 splicing
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Post-transcriptional control of mitochondrial nad7 splicing
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Loss of BIR6 impairs NAD7 maturation and complex I function
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: bir6-2 suppresses mod1 growth defects, ROS accumulation and cell death; also compromises HR and bacterial resistance
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Complex I deficiency caused by bir6-2 is sufficient to reduce mitochondrial ROS-dependent mod1 PCD
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bir6-2, a complex I-deficient mutant, suppresses mod1 phenotypes. 2. BIR6 is a PPR regulator affecting NAD7 intron 1 splicing. 3. Reduced complex I function correlates with reduced ROS accumulation and weaker HR/disease resistance.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic crossing with mod1; phenotype analysis; ROS staining; prior functional assignment cited in paper; HR and bacterial growth assays
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pathogen infection with Pst DC3000 carrying avrRpt2 or avrRps4
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Affects mitochondrial complex I through NAD7 RNA processing
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for dissecting mitochondrial ROS-PCD links, but undesirable for crop improvement because defense is compromised.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced complex I activity and increased susceptibility to bacteria; attenuated HR.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core experimentally tested suppressor in Results even though mechanistic characterization relied partly on prior reports.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ROS signal for programmed cell death and immunity
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Mitochondrial complex I biogenesis via nad1/nad2/nad4 splicing
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Loss of nMAT1 impairs splicing of mitochondria-encoded complex I transcripts
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: nMat1-1 suppresses mod1 growth defects, ROS accumulation and cell death; deficiency also attenuates HR and bacterial resistance
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Complex I impairment via transcript splicing defects lowers ROS production required for mod1 PCD
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nMat1-1 suppresses mod1 phenotypes. 2. nMAT1 is a complex I biogenesis regulator affecting nad1/nad2/nad4 splicing. 3. Complex I-deficient nMat1 plants show reduced HR and pathogen resistance.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic crossing with mod1; phenotype analysis; ROS staining; HR and bacterial growth assays
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pathogen infection with Pst DC3000 strains
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Required for proper biogenesis of mitochondrial complex I
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Mainly mechanistic value; reduced respiration/defense makes it unattractive for deployment.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Compromised complex I, weaker HR, higher bacterial susceptibility.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core suppressor gene function in this paper is inferred through mutant analysis combined with prior published molecular role.
